text_clean: This is my third time here and let me tell you there’s been tons of changes. After you get to Aguas Calientes, everyone whether you hired a guide or not must now go to the Ministerio de Culturas and stand in line just to get a number to return later that night or the next day. My understanding from talking to other tour guides is that tour guides used to be able to buy your tickets and they would buy at the Peruvian National price and resell at the international tourist price. To cut down on this corruption now everyone must go and present their passports or their proof of residency (DNI). You are asked how many people in your party and you are given individual numbers for each person. You cannot show up early as they go by the number you were given. Their computer systems are not very stable so expect delays. We were in line a little over 2 hours in the morning and over 3 hours in the evening so almost 6 hours. When your number is called, you go upstairs and have to wait in and move yourself along the winding chairs that lead you back down the stairs to the cashier windows to be asked what circuit you will be getting. There are limited number of tickets per circuit and even though there are “maps” there is nothing showing you what you will see in each circuit and what your pictures will look like. There are screens that can be looked to determine which circuits are still available as you approach the window to place your order. Fee per permit/ticket is $50/person for international traveler regardless of the circuit chosen. Circuit 2A/B is quite popular so for me, I had to go with circuit 3A and that’s the picture I am sharing on this review. Previously on my other 2 visits, I was able to do the whole trip as a day trip and see Machu Picchu and return to Cusco the same day. Now you must stay overnight and some people up to two nights so plan carefully. That said, this area is a lower altitude than Cusco so it is easier to breathe and the town is much more quaint and more enjoyable. That said do not buy your return tickets to Cusco until you have your tour time because you are only allowed to make changes up to 24 hours before and if you try to change at 23 hours and 59 minutes, you will have to buy new tickets. Another piece of advice, when booking bus/train tickets go with Peru Rail instead of Inca Rail as the train cars are much nicer and have wider aisles and better “shows”. With these changes, it almost makes it not worth it to even visit. To me, Machu Picchu is completely overrated and overpriced and inconvenient to get to. Although I’m glad I’ve come 3 times (first time 2021 during covid and raining so pictures were not adequate, second time 2023 with niece and third time 2025 with my wife because she wanted to visit), I’ve thought this every time I’ve come.

text_clean: It was nice, not even close to my favorite thing in Peru or my favorite trek but I would say definitely worth two to three days if you have time and are in the arequipa area. It is a very developed area for tourism so expect it to be busy and to have to pay for camping. I was recommended to take 5 litres of water, I took 6, and I did drink around 4, the reason I am included this however is that there were multiple locations where I could have refilled with my filter but didn't need to, therefore unless you drink a very large amount of water I wouldn't bring 6 litres. This is of course in the part of the canyon I did which started near cabanaconde.

text_clean: A wonder del mundo, Machu Picchu is worth the long journey (flight to Lima, another flight to Cusco, taxi ride to Ollantaytambo, train ride to Aguas Calientes, and sunrise bus up to the entrance of Machu Picchu). Note that there are many prohibito items and there will be multiple pasaporte and boleto checks at the entrance and near the beginning of the path. Circuito 2 with the Inca bridge has an additional ranger patrolling the trail to the bridge, with a sign-in and sign-out process to control the number of visitors in the narrow area. A guide is technically mandatory for the entire park but is not rigidly enforced. However, it is recommended to arrange a trusted guia from the hotel rather than randomly picking one at the parada de autobus or entrada. My private guide charged 150 soles total for two people ($40 USD) and spent 3.5 hours from meeting at my hotel until leaving us at the area close to the salida (allowing us to stay and watch the llamas). Guides can help to take pictures of their clients at prime spots while explaining key details of the archeological history. Other than Inca bridge, there is no enforcement of the 4-hour total time limit through the complete circuit, though technically there is a one-way direction. Beware of biting insects (I washed my hands after applying a spray at the hotel and I received multiple bites from sand flies). The temperature is cool before sunrise but once the sun breaks through the fog and the view of the mountain is clear, the sunlight is bright and powerful. It's important to dress in layers and watch every step!

Guides can help to take pictures of their clients at prime spots while explaining key details of the archeological history. Other than Inca bridge, there is no enforcement of the 4-hour total time limit through the complete circuit, though technically there is a one-way direction. Beware of biting insects (I washed my hands after applying a spray at the hotel and I received multiple bites from sand flies). The temperature is cool before sunrise but once the sun breaks through the fog and the view of the mountain is clear, the sunlight is bright and powerful. It's important to dress in layers and watch every step!
label: positivo

Note that there are many prohibito items and there will be multiple pasaporte and boleto checks at the entrance and near the beginning of the path. Circuito 2 with the Inca bridge has an additional ranger patrolling the trail to the bridge, with a sign-in and sign-out process to control the number of visitors in the narrow area. text_clean: 1) YOU CANNOT WALK BACK, OR REPEAT ANY PART OF YOUR CIRCUIT. There are staff watching and they will blow their whistle and shout at you if you try. We arrived in a downpour so we walked to some shelter and waited it out. When we tried to walk back on the circuit we had paid for, we were not allowed. It doesn't matter that it's not busy, you want to take photos or any other reason, they will not let you walk back, even 20m. Unfortunately this was not clear when we purchased the ticket so we missed a significant portion of our route :( Therefore I'd advise you to walk slowly and enjoy the views, because once you arrive at the exit, that will be the end of your time at macchu pichu. I feel the staff lack compassion since it was 7am and quiet at macchu pichu, and I didn't want it to rain. If they had a cafe inside at the beginning I'd have waited there, but you have to enter at your time slot so I was left in a downpour in my poncho. I'd paid for flights, accomodation, and so many buses and trains to be there. The staff didn't show any compassion for my situation and were curt. <- therefore one star, but then I added one star for the staff that let me go take pictures of the llamas :) 2) TOILETS WILL COST YOU EXTRA ON TOP OF THE ENTRANCE FEE, AND BAGGAGE STORAGE. Which I also find ridiculous considering the entrance ticket is so expensive. Toilet S/2 and baggage is S/5. 3) YOU CANNOT ENTER BEFORE THE ENTRANCE TIME ON YOUR TICKET Something to do with the system they use. 4) DO NOT FLY A DRONE The staff have a walkie talkie system and respond very quickly. I don't know what the consequences are, but the people we saw were taken to the office. 5) DO NOT BOOK EARLY MORNING TIME SLOT IF YOU ARE VISITING IN WET/ SHOULDER SEASONS The clouds tend to hang low in the sky in the morning so you might not have the best view/ you might have to wait for clouds to clear. 6) YOU CAN BUY THE BUS TICKET FOR THE BUS UPWARDS ONLINE, BEFOREHAND (12 USD per ride) Just look for the one bus company that runs the route. I can't remember off the top of my head. You can also purchase them at the bottom and at the top, the process is quick and easy. Hope this helps :) I did route 1&2 and was happy with all the areas it covered.

text_clean: El cañon del colca tiene un paisaje imponente e inmenso, mucha flora y fauna. Hay que ir con buen calzado y un nivel intermedio de estado físico ya que hay que hacer trekking en altura y resulta medio dificultoso si no estás acostumbrado. Es viaje es bastante extenso, más o menos son 2 o 3 horas para llegar. No tuve la suerte de ver ningún Cóndor pero fue una linda experiencia. Esta excursión no la recomiendo hacer si vas a Perú con los días contados, hay lugares mejores.

Some good examples set up with mannequins to explain the way it was built and about their culture.

text_clean: Una advertencia muy seria y real es que acá casi todo turista que visita Perú llega a este sitio que no es nada barato empezando con lo absurdamente caro que es el Tren que es el único medio (práctico y rápido) para llegar a Aguascalientes cuyo pasaje de ida y vuelta fácilmente te gastas 160 dólares, la entrada se tiene que comprar en línea con al menos 40 días de anticipación y el transporte de subida y bajada a las ruinas cuesta 24 dólares, no se gastarán menos de 220 dólares, lo terrible de esto es que el lugar siempre está atestado de gente se hacen largas filas para comprar pasajes de el bus, para subirse en el para regresarse, fila para entrar y ahí se te pueden ir fácilmente 4 horas si 4 horas, el clima es traiciónero en cualquier momento llueve y te frusta meses de planeación, por otro lados la Cuidad Perdida es simplemente subreal 😍

text_clean: Las telecabinas son de última generación, es un paseo bastante interesante. Pero la fortaleza está aún muy lejos de ser un punto turístico como Machu Picchu. Hay que caminar sobre tierra, rocas y lodo para poder llegar a la cima en donde verán la fortaleza desde afuera porque no se puede ingresar al complejo. La caminata dura aproximadamente 2 horas y es muy difícil para niños y adultos mayores, incluso personas que no tienen mucha resistencia física.

They bank turn, bank left to allow passengers on both sides to see it. We went with Aerodiana, nice plane in good condition. Lucky I read about the other unsafe airlines and emergency landings after my ride or would have been a nervous wreck during the flight. Was lucky had a beautiful sunny clear day and smooth ride (aside from the purpose banking, poor kid on our plane threw up. The plane had plenty of bags for situations like these 😂)

text_clean: Arequipa is an incredible experience overall and surprisingly affordable! Known as the White City, Arequipa boasts charming architecture and a vibrant culture that makes every visit special. One of the highlights of my trip was visiting the breathtaking Colca Canyon. The landscapes of Colca Canyon are absolutely unbelievable. The vast, rugged beauty of the canyon leaves you in awe, and it's a fantastic place to connect with nature. The tranquility and peacefulness you find there are unmatched. It's a perfect escape from the hustle and bustle of daily life. Walking along the canyon, you can truly appreciate the serene environment and the stunning views that stretch as far as the eye can see. Watching the majestic condors glide through the air is an experience in itself. Arequipa adds to the experience with its unique blend of historical sites and modern amenities. The combination of the city's unique charm and the breathtaking beauty of Colca Canyon makes for a memorable visit. Whether you're hiking the trails, soaking in the hot springs, or simply taking in the views, it's a place that invites you to relax and unwind. Overall, visiting Colca Canyon in Arequipa is an unforgettable experience that I highly recommend. The natural beauty, peaceful atmosphere, and affordability make it a must-see destination for anyone looking to explore the wonders of Peru.

text_clean: Cuando este parque se creó incluía poder disfrutar de todas las piletas por una sola entrada. El alcalde que lo diseñó pensó en el relajo de la rutina y un regalo a la población. text_clean: Fui al circuito 1-C el 17 de abril a las 8:00 am y me tocó lluvia y niebla. No obstante despeja por momentos y se puede apreciar la vista desde la casa del guardian al que se tiene acceso, pero por la condición climática descarté la subida que ofrecía la ruta porque no valía la pena. Mejor reservar para entrar al medio día, vas a la fija. Aunque el paisaje es encantador por las montañas, incluso desde el tren, me pareció que tanto sacrificio y dinero para llegar hasta allí está un poco sobrevalorado. Demasiados turistas.

text_clean: Machu Picchu itself is certainly an impressive archeological site. The price (entry ticket per circuit 152S, resp. 50$) is very high in my regards, especially since there is zero information given on the site itself. Further all means of transport are overpriced compared to the rest of Peru and there is no alternative. Overall a nice experience, but felt overpriced, without any reasonable alternative for means of transport, too little/no information and thus a overrated.

text_clean: Una de las maravillas del mundo Antiguo Machu Picchu es una de las joyas arqueológicas mas espectaculares del mundo 🤩. Cuenta con baños por varios puntos del monasterio, un bar y alguna tienda. Fuimos un sábado sobre las 10:00 y no había mucha gente, pero igualmente al ser tan grande no hay aglomeraciones. Muy recomendable

text_clean: We weren't sure if it would fit in our schedule for Peru at first, but I'm very glad we made a detour and planned some extra time for this. Absolutely stunning views and very varied flora and fauna. A few notes: the local authority Autocolca doesn't do their bookkeeping very well and I don't recommend getting E-tickets. Get your tickets in the early morning from their office in Chivay (plaza des armas) as you have the best chance of spotting the mighty condor in the morning, and they don't do much work there in the afternoon. Also I do not recommend going over the S109 (south south west) unless you have plenty of time (travel time with google maps is spot-on though) a sturdy car and good driving skills, because the road is undergoing about 100kms of heavy construction with poor driving conditions.

text_clean: I came to Arequipa to enjoy the city and to visit the Monastery was something I was excited about doing. I arrived yesterday and I was extremely disappointed to find that there was no way for someone like me to enter and enjoy this beautiful piece of architecture and history.. I have muscular dystrophy and to use stairs is basically impossible for me. I understand that it is a historic city and things sometimes can’t be changed for preservation purposes - but to have no ramp or mini platform elevator or alternate entrance or even a handrail for the stairs was shocking and disappointing to say the least. I only write this review to let others know who may be physically altered (senior citizens or pregnant women) or handicapped like me that this will be what you find when you arrive there. It makes no sense for a place that is so historical and beautiful and important, a tourist treasure of the city, to not be accesible to everyone. I was still able to enjoy some of the beautiful architecture from the outside, but it was very sad not to be able to experience this.

text_clean: Disappointing :-( First of all, my experience has nothing to do with the actual site. The view, ruins, landscape and llamas are surreal! My disappointing experience is about the management of the site. I knew it was a touristy place and quite expensive to go. Most places are restricted/controlled and you can't enter inside the temples etc..you can just go around. Apparently a few years ago everything was open (said some friends who went before). And then you have all the tour groups that take a lot of spaces on the path. I almost didn't take any pictures, I didn't feel I had the freedom to do so because the path goes only on one way (so you can't go back on your path) and people are moving so you kind of feel a bit in a tour group without being in it and you kind of have to go forward. I didn't feel free to walk around/take pictures as I would have liked to. And then there are 4 choices of tickets, with different circuits, bit it doesn't give you access to everywhere with only one ticket, so you need to buy another one. This wasn't very cleared on the website. I just find that you put so much effort (physically, mentally and financially) to get there (waking up super early, doing all the trip to get to Aguas Calientes, walking up the (many!) stairs to the entrance, and the cost etc), in the end to leave with a bitter feeling. Is it worth it? I'm still unsure if it was.

text_clean: I really hate 1 star reviews for this historical site but I am writing one. Due to the protests in September of 2025 we were unable to get our train to Aguas Caliente. I completely support people's right to protest and was unwilling to travel to MP during this dispute. Some people travelled by van for 5 hours and walked for several more miles to access the town. I chose to stay in Ollantaytambo for a couple of extra days which was really enjoyable. I am giving MP 1 star because despite the issues people have accessing MP their refund process is impossible. I was told I need to submit personal information including pictures of my passport. They sent me a link to do this. The link does not say anything about the purpose of why I am submitting this information. I am wary of providing this information without a guarantee of getting a refund. The ticketing website has no information about asking for refunds. Surely they are aware that thousands of people were unable to go to MP due to the strike. Perurail actually sent an email explaining the refund process. I imagine the ministry of tourism made quite a bit of money from no-show tickets.
